enrichment.short_description: CKAN is a free, open-source data management system for building open data portals, used by governments and organizations worldwide to publish, search, and share datasets.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CKAN is a free, open-source data catalog and portal platform that governments and organizations worldwide use to publish, search, and share open datasets.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CKAN (Comprehensive Knowledge Archive Network) is a free, open-source data management system used to build and run data catalog and open-data portal websites. It originated from work by economist Rufus Pollock and the Open Knowledge Foundation (OKF), a UK non-profit founded in Cambridge in 2004 and now headquartered in London; the first public CKAN release dates to 2006, and the codebase is still maintained by OKF with a broad community of contributors. CKAN is written primarily in Python (Flask/Pylons), stores metadata in PostgreSQL, and uses Apache Solr for search indexing. It is distributed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPL-3.0), so the software itself carries no license fee. Organizations self-host CKAN on their own servers or cloud infrastructure, or pay third-party vendors such as Datopian, Link Digital, or Keitaro for managed hosting, migration, and support contracts; there is no official hosted SaaS product from OKF itself. CKAN's core function is letting publishers register datasets with structured metadata, upload or link to data files (CSV, Excel, GeoJSON, PDF, and more), and expose that data through a searchable, filterable web catalog plus a RESTful JSON Action API that developers use to query, retrieve, and programmatically manage datasets and organizations. Built-in capabilities include dataset versioning and revision history, resource previews and lightweight visualizations (bar, line, and pie charts, and maps for tabular or geospatial resources), user and organization management with role-based access control, and activity streams for tracking changes. A modular extension system, with more than 60 community-maintained extensions, adds capabilities such as web harvesting from other catalogs, a DataStore and DataPusher for making tabular data queryable via API, analytics, custom theming, and federation so multiple CKAN instances can share and aggregate datasets across a network of portals. CKAN is best known as the software running large government open-data catalogs, including data.gov (United States), data.gov.uk (United Kingdom), open.canada.ca, and numerous city, state, national, and international data portals, as well as humanitarian and research-data platforms such as the Humanitarian Data Exchange (data.humdata.org). Its practical audience is government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, and organizations that need to publish structured datasets to the public or internal stakeholders in a searchable, standardized way — not individual consumers or small businesses. Because CKAN requires server administration, database management, and Solr search-index tuning (particularly at scale), it generally needs a technical team or a managed-hosting vendor to operate day to day. Pricing is therefore best understood as free software plus paid infrastructure and optional support: total cost of ownership depends on hosting and vendor choices rather than any fee charged by the CKAN project. Adjacent or competing tools include Socrata, OpenDataSoft, and DKAN (a Drupal-based, CKAN-inspired alternative), but CKAN remains one of the most widely deployed open-source options for institutional open-data publishing because of its maturity, its API, and its adoption across major national data portals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CKAN is a free, open-source data management system built to power open-data portals and data catalogs. Originating from the Open Knowledge Foundation in the mid-2000s, it has become one of the most widely used platforms behind government open-data initiatives, including data.gov and data.gov.uk.

Rather than being sold as a hosted product, CKAN is self-hosted software: organizations install it on their own infrastructure or work with a third-party vendor for managed hosting and support. It is written in Python, backed by PostgreSQL for storage and Solr for search, and released under the AGPL-3.0 open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, CKAN lets publishers register datasets with rich metadata, attach or link data files in formats like CSV, Excel, GeoJSON, and PDF, and expose everything through a searchable, filterable public catalog.

A RESTful JSON Action API gives developers programmatic access to datasets, organizations, and search, making CKAN suitable for automated pipelines and third-party integrations rather than just manual browsing.

Extensions — more than 60 are publicly available — add capabilities such as harvesting datasets from other catalogs, a queryable DataStore for tabular data, analytics, custom theming, and federation between multiple CKAN portals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CKAN's software has no license fee: the source code is free to download, modify, and self-host under the AGPL-3.0 license. The real cost of running CKAN comes from infrastructure (servers, database, search index) and, optionally, support or managed hosting contracts from vendors such as Datopian or Link Digital.

There is no official paid tier or managed SaaS offering from the CKAN project or the Open Knowledge Foundation, so organizations evaluating CKAN should budget for either in-house DevOps capacity or a third-party hosting partner.

features.title: Dataset catalog & search
features.description: A searchable, filterable web catalog where datasets are registered with structured metadata, tags, and organization ownership.

features.title: RESTful Action API
features.description: A JSON API for programmatically creating, querying, and managing datasets, resources, and organizations.

features.title: Resource previews & visualizations
features.description: Built-in previews and simple charts (bar, line, pie) and maps for tabular and geospatial data resources.

features.title: DataStore & DataPusher
features.description: Extensions that load tabular data into a queryable database so it can be filtered and accessed via API, not just downloaded.

features.title: Extension ecosystem
features.description: Over 60 community-built extensions covering harvesting, analytics, theming, authentication, and more.

features.title: Federated portals
features.description: Multiple CKAN instances can share and aggregate datasets across a network of connected portals.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: User and organization management with configurable permissions for publishing and editing datasets.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Mature, widely adopted by national government open-data portals
Strong, well-documented API for automation and integrations
Highly extensible via a large plugin ecosystem
cons: Requires server administration and database/search-index expertise to self-host
No official managed SaaS product directly from the CKAN project
User interface is functional but dated compared to modern commercial data-catalog tools
Deployment and upgrades can be technically demanding at larger scale

faqs.question: Is CKAN free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. CKAN is open-source software released under the AGPL-3.0 license, so there is no fee to download, modify, or self-host it. Costs come from hosting infrastructure and any optional third-party support.

faqs.question: Who typically uses CKAN?
faqs.answer: Government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, and other organizations that need to publish structured datasets publicly or internally, including operators of national open-data portals such as data.gov and data.gov.uk.

faqs.question: Does CKAN offer official hosting?
faqs.answer: The CKAN project itself does not sell hosting. Organizations either self-host on their own infrastructure or contract third-party vendors, such as Datopian or Link Digital, for managed CKAN hosting and support.

faqs.question: Can CKAN be customized?
faqs.answer: Yes. CKAN has a modular extension system with more than 60 publicly available extensions covering harvesting, visualization, theming, and more, and organizations can also build custom extensions.

faqs.question: What technical stack does CKAN use?
faqs.answer: CKAN is built in Python, uses PostgreSQL for data storage, and uses Apache Solr for search indexing.

faqs.question: Is CKAN suitable for a small team without DevOps resources?
faqs.answer: It can be challenging without technical expertise, since self-hosting requires managing servers, a database, and a search index; smaller teams often use a managed-hosting vendor instead.

use_cases.title: Government open-data portals
use_cases.description: Publishing public-sector datasets in a searchable catalog with standardized metadata and an API for reuse.
use_cases.audience: Government agencies and public-sector data teams

use_cases.title: Research data repositories
use_cases.description: Cataloging and sharing research datasets with structured metadata and version tracking across institutions.

use_cases.title: Humanitarian and NGO data sharing
use_cases.description: Aggregating and distributing datasets across humanitarian organizations, as used by platforms like the Humanitarian Data Exchange.

features: Full CKAN source code and core platform
Community support via GitHub and forums
Requires own server and hosting infrastructure

enrichment.short_description: Clair is a free, open-source vulnerability scanner that statically analyzes container images against CVE databases to secure registries and CI/CD pipelines.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, US (originally CoreOS; now maintained under Red Hat)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clair is a free, open-source tool that statically scans container images for known vulnerabilities, powering security checks for registries like Quay.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clair is a free, open-source vulnerability scanner for container images, originally announced by CoreOS in November 2015 and later brought under Red Hat's stewardship following Red Hat's 2018 acquisition of CoreOS. The project is hosted in the quay/clair repository on GitHub and is licensed under Apache 2.0, meaning there is no cost to download, run, or modify it. Clair works by statically analyzing the contents of OCI and Docker container images: it indexes the packages and libraries present in each image layer, then matches those packages against vulnerability data pulled continuously from sources such as the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) and distribution-specific feeds covering Red Hat Enterprise Linux, Alpine, Ubuntu, Debian, Oracle Linux, Photon OS, and SUSE, as well as language ecosystems like Java, Python, Go, and JavaScript. Architecturally, Clair v4 is split into cooperating microservices — an indexer that parses image layers, a matcher that compares indexed packages against known vulnerability data, a notifier that fires webhooks when previously scanned images gain newly discovered CVE matches, and an API layer exposing REST and gRPC endpoints — though it can also run in a more monolithic mode for smaller deployments, CI pipelines, or air-gapped/disconnected environments. Clair's most prominent real-world use is as the vulnerability-scanning backend for Red Hat Quay and the public Quay.io container registry, where it continuously re-checks stored images as new vulnerabilities are published so that image owners get an up-to-date view of their security exposure without re-uploading anything. It has also been used by other security tooling in the container ecosystem, such as StackRox, and is commonly integrated into CI/CD pipelines to fail builds or flag images that contain known-vulnerable packages before they reach production. Because Clair is infrastructure software rather than a consumer application, it has no subscription tiers, seats, or account system of its own; organizations run it themselves (typically alongside a container registry) or consume it indirectly through a product like Quay that embeds it. There is no official managed cloud version of Clair sold directly by its maintainers, so adopting it requires the same kind of operational investment as any self-hosted open-source security tool: provisioning the microservices, connecting a datastore, and keeping vulnerability feeds updated. Its target users are platform engineering, DevSecOps, and security teams responsible for container image supply-chain security, rather than individual developers looking for a point-and-click scanner. Clair competes with tools such as Trivy, Grype, and Anchore Engine in the open-source container-scanning space, and with commercial platforms like Snyk Container or Prisma Cloud for organizations wanting a fully managed alternative; Clair's differentiators are its deep integration with the Quay registry ecosystem, its Apache 2.0 licensing, and its long operational track record at the scale of a public container registry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clair is a free, open-source vulnerability scanner for container images. It was first announced by CoreOS in November 2015 and is now maintained as Project Clair, primarily developed within the quay/clair GitHub organization under Red Hat's stewardship after Red Hat's 2018 acquisition of CoreOS.

Clair performs static analysis: it inspects the packages and libraries inside a container image and checks them against continuously updated vulnerability databases, rather than executing the container to observe runtime behavior.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clair indexes container image layers to identify installed packages across major Linux distributions (RHEL, Alpine, Ubuntu, Debian, Oracle Linux, Photon OS, SUSE) and common language ecosystems (Java, Python, Go, JavaScript), then matches them against known-vulnerability data.

Its v4 architecture separates concerns into an indexer, a matcher, a notifier (for webhook-based alerts on new CVE matches against previously scanned images), and a REST/gRPC API, and it can be deployed as microservices, as a more monolithic setup, or embedded in CI pipelines and disconnected environments.
Clair is the vulnerability-scanning engine behind Red Hat Quay and the public Quay.io registry, continuously re-evaluating stored images as new vulnerabilities become known.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clair is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no cost to download, deploy, or modify. There is no official paid or managed version sold directly by the project.
Organizations that don't want to operate Clair themselves typically consume it indirectly through a product that embeds it, such as Red Hat Quay, which has its own commercial and hosted offerings.

features.title: Static image analysis
features.description: Scans container image layers for installed packages without executing the container.

features.title: Multi-distribution CVE matching
features.description: Checks packages against vulnerability data covering RHEL, Alpine, Ubuntu, Debian, Oracle Linux, Photon OS, and SUSE.

features.title: Language ecosystem coverage
features.description: Detects vulnerable dependencies in Java, Python, Go, and JavaScript packages inside images.

features.title: Continuous re-scanning
features.description: Re-checks previously indexed images against newly published vulnerabilities without requiring a fresh upload.

features.title: Webhook notifications
features.description: The notifier component fires webhooks when a scanned image gains new CVE matches.

features.title: REST and gRPC API
features.description: Exposes indexing and matching functionality programmatically for integration into registries and pipelines.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Runs as microservices, a monolith, in CI pipelines, or in disconnected/air-gapped environments.

pros: Free and open source under Apache 2.0
Proven at scale as the scanning engine behind Quay.io
Broad coverage across major Linux distributions and language ecosystems
Flexible deployment options, including air-gapped environments
cons: No official managed cloud version; requires self-hosting and operational investment
Setup and datastore management require DevOps/security engineering expertise
No built-in dashboard for non-technical users beyond what integrating tools provide
Best experienced when paired with a registry like Quay rather than used fully standalone

faqs.question: Is Clair free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clair is open-source software licensed under Apache 2.0, so it can be downloaded, run, and modified at no cost.

faqs.question: What does Clair actually scan?
faqs.answer: Clair statically analyzes container image layers, identifying installed OS packages and language dependencies, then matches them against vulnerability databases covering multiple Linux distributions and language ecosystems.

faqs.question: Who maintains Clair today?
faqs.answer: Clair originated at CoreOS and has been maintained primarily under Red Hat's stewardship since Red Hat acquired CoreOS in 2018; development happens in the open on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Clair require a container registry like Quay?
faqs.answer: No, Clair can run standalone via its API, but it is most commonly deployed as the scanning backend for a registry, most notably Red Hat Quay and Quay.io.

faqs.question: Can Clair be used in CI/CD pipelines?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clair's API-driven, microservice-friendly design makes it suitable for integration into CI/CD pipelines to flag or block images with known vulnerabilities.

use_cases.title: Container registry security scanning
use_cases.description: Acting as the scanning backend for a private or public container registry, continuously checking stored images for known CVEs.

use_cases.title: CI/CD pipeline security gates
use_cases.description: Scanning images during the build pipeline to catch known-vulnerable packages before deployment.

features: Self-hosted deployment as microservices or monolith
No usage limits imposed by the project itself

enrichment.short_description: Clari is an enterprise revenue orchestration platform that unifies pipeline forecasting, deal inspection, conversation intelligence, and sales execution for B2B teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clari is a revenue orchestration platform combining AI forecasting, pipeline inspection, and conversation intelligence for enterprise B2B sales teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clari is an enterprise revenue operations platform, founded in 2013 by Andy Byrne and Venkat Rangan and headquartered in Sunnyvale, California. The company positions itself as a "revenue orchestration" platform that connects forecasting, pipeline management, deal execution, and go-to-market analytics into a single system used by enterprise sales, RevOps, and executive teams to run recurring revenue processes. Clari has raised significant venture funding over multiple rounds, including a $225 million Series F led by Blackstone and Silver Lake in January 2022 that valued the company at roughly $2.6 billion, and industry estimates put its annual recurring revenue in the hundreds of millions of dollars with roughly 1,500+ enterprise customers as of the mid-2020s. The product is organized into several modules that can be purchased together or, in some cases, separately: Clari Forecast (the original core product) provides AI-assisted revenue forecasting, pipeline inspection, and deal-risk scoring pulled from CRM and email/calendar data; Clari Copilot (formerly Wingman, acquired by Clari) adds conversation intelligence, call recording, transcription, and AI-generated coaching and deal insights from sales calls; Clari Align supports mutual action plans and buyer-seller collaboration workspaces to keep complex deals on track; and Clari Groove (from Clari's acquisition of sales-engagement platform Groove) adds email/call cadences and outbound automation. Clari does not publish self-serve pricing; every deployment is a custom, quote-based enterprise sale that depends on which modules are purchased, seat count, and negotiated terms, with third-party analyses estimating typical per-user costs in roughly the $60-$125/user/month range per module (and combined-stack costs in the $200-$310/user/month range for large deployments), plus separate implementation and professional-services fees that can run from roughly $15,000 to $75,000 for enterprise rollouts. Clari integrates with major CRM systems (principally Salesforce), email and calendar platforms, and various sales-engagement and communication tools, and it is built for large sales organizations rather than solo sellers or small teams — the platform's core value proposition is giving revenue leaders a single, always-current view of pipeline health and forecast accuracy that would otherwise be assembled manually from spreadsheets and disparate CRM reports. Because pricing and implementation scope are highly variable and negotiated per account, prospective buyers typically need to go through a sales and demo process with Clari directly to get an accurate quote, and the platform is generally evaluated against competitors such as Gong, Salesforce (via Sales Cloud/Einstein), Aviso, and BoostUp in the revenue-intelligence and forecasting category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clari is an enterprise revenue orchestration platform founded in 2013 by Andy Byrne and Venkat Rangan and headquartered in Sunnyvale, California. It combines forecasting, pipeline inspection, conversation intelligence, and deal-execution tools into a single system for large B2B sales organizations.

The company has raised substantial venture funding, including a 2022 Series F round led by Blackstone and Silver Lake that valued the business at approximately $2.6 billion, and serves enterprise customers across industries that rely on predictable, forecastable recurring revenue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clari Forecast, the platform's original core module, uses AI-assisted analysis of CRM, email, and calendar data to surface deal risk and produce more accurate, less manual revenue forecasts than spreadsheet-based rollups.

Clari Copilot adds conversation intelligence — call recording, transcription, and AI-generated summaries and coaching — while Clari Align provides shared mutual action plans for buyer-seller collaboration on complex deals, and Clari Groove adds outbound email/call cadence automation.

The platform integrates with major CRMs (principally Salesforce) and sales communication tools, giving revenue leaders a consolidated, continuously updated view of pipeline health rather than a static end-of-quarter report.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clari does not publish self-serve pricing. Every deployment is quoted individually based on which modules (Forecast, Copilot, Align, Groove) are purchased, seat count, and negotiated enterprise terms.

Third-party pricing analyses suggest per-module costs commonly fall in the $60-$125 per user, per month range, with combined multi-module deployments running higher, plus separate implementation and professional-services fees for enterprise rollouts.

features.title: AI-assisted forecasting
features.description: Analyzes CRM, email, and calendar activity to produce revenue forecasts and flag at-risk deals.

features.title: Pipeline inspection
features.description: Gives managers and executives a real-time, rollup view of pipeline health across teams and regions.

features.title: Conversation intelligence (Copilot)
features.description: Records, transcribes, and analyzes sales calls to surface deal risk, competitor mentions, and coaching opportunities.

features.title: Mutual action plans (Align)
features.description: Shared workspaces where sellers and buyers track next steps, timelines, and requirements together.

features.title: Sales engagement automation (Groove)
features.description: Email and call cadence automation for outbound prospecting and pipeline generation.

features.title: CRM integration
features.description: Syncs with Salesforce and other systems so forecast and pipeline data stay current without manual entry.

features.title: RevOps analytics
features.description: Dashboards and reporting built for revenue operations and executive-level visibility into the sales funnel.

pros: Consolidates forecasting, deal inspection, and conversation intelligence in one platform
Strong fit for large, complex enterprise sales organizations
AI-driven deal-risk scoring reduces reliance on manual spreadsheet forecasting
Backed by significant funding and a large existing enterprise customer base
cons: No public pricing; requires a sales process to get a quote
Can be costly at scale, especially when combining multiple modules
Implementation and professional-services costs add to total cost of ownership
Primarily built for enterprise teams, less suited to small sales organizations

faqs.question: How much does Clari cost?
faqs.answer: Clari does not publish pricing publicly. Costs depend on which modules (Forecast, Copilot, Align, Groove) are purchased, seat count, and negotiated enterprise terms, so buyers need to request a quote.

faqs.question: What is Clari Copilot?
faqs.answer: Clari Copilot is Clari's conversation-intelligence product, providing call recording, transcription, and AI-generated summaries and coaching insights from sales calls.

faqs.question: Does Clari integrate with Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clari integrates with major CRM platforms, principally Salesforce, syncing pipeline and deal data to power its forecasting and inspection features.

faqs.question: Who is Clari built for?
faqs.answer: Clari is built for enterprise B2B sales, revenue operations, and executive teams that need consolidated forecasting and pipeline visibility, rather than individual sellers or small teams.

faqs.question: When was Clari founded?
faqs.answer: Clari was founded in 2013 by Andy Byrne and Venkat Rangan and is headquartered in Sunnyvale, California.

use_cases.description: Replacing manual, spreadsheet-based forecast rollups with AI-assisted, continuously updated forecasts.
use_cases.audience: Sales leadership and revenue operations teams

use_cases.title: Sales call coaching

use_cases.title: Complex enterprise deal management

use_cases.audience: Enterprise account executives and buying committees

enrichment.short_description: Claude is Anthropic's family of AI assistant models and apps for conversation, coding, research, and agentic tasks, available via web, desktop, mobile, and API.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Claude is Anthropic's AI assistant for chat, coding, and research, offering a free tier plus Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans and a usage-based API.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Claude is the AI assistant product family built by Anthropic, an AI safety and research company founded in January 2021 by siblings Dario Amodei (CEO) and Daniela Amodei (President), along with several other former OpenAI researchers, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Anthropic operates as a public benefit corporation and has grown rapidly since its founding, raising large funding rounds and reaching a valuation reported around several hundred billion dollars in 2026, with an estimated headcount in the low thousands. Claude is accessible through a free consumer web and mobile chat app, dedicated desktop and mobile apps, a developer-facing API (available directly through Anthropic's platform as well as through Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI), and enterprise integrations. Anthropic offers multiple Claude model families spanning different capability and cost tiers — commonly described as smaller/faster models optimized for speed and cost, and larger/more capable models optimized for complex reasoning, coding, and long-running agentic tasks — and these models are used both directly in Claude's own apps and by third-party developers building on the API. Consumer pricing, per Anthropic's official pricing page, includes a Free plan ($0) offering web and mobile chat, code generation, content creation, web search, memory, and file creation with usage limits; a Pro plan priced at $17/month billed annually (or $20/month billed monthly) that adds higher usage limits, access to Claude Code (Anthropic's agentic coding tool), unlimited projects, research features, and access to multiple Claude models; and a Max plan starting at $100/month offering substantially higher usage caps (described as 5x or 20x Pro-level usage), higher output limits, and priority access during periods of high demand. For organizations, Clari-style seat-based Team plans are priced at $20-25/month per standard seat (annual vs. monthly billing) or $100-125/month per premium seat, covering teams of roughly 2 to 150 people with centralized billing, admin controls, and SSO; Enterprise plans are custom-quoted based on seat count plus API usage and add SCIM provisioning, audit logs, role-based access control, configurable data retention, HIPAA-ready options, IP allowlisting, and a compliance API. Beyond the chat interface, Anthropic has built out an ecosystem of specialized product surfaces under the Claude brand — including Claude Code for terminal- and IDE-based agentic software development, and integrations with tools like Microsoft 365 — reflecting a broader push from single-turn chat toward multi-step, tool-using "agentic" workflows that can read files, run code, browse the web, and act across a session rather than just answer one prompt at a time. Claude's primary audience spans individual knowledge workers and developers using the free or Pro tiers for everyday writing, research, and coding assistance; power users and small teams on Max or Team plans running heavier workloads or needing collaboration features; and enterprises building regulated or large-scale AI applications through custom Enterprise agreements or direct API usage, where cost is governed by input/output token consumption per model rather than a flat subscription. Anthropic differentiates Claude primarily on its safety-focused development approach, long-context handling, coding and agentic-task performance, and enterprise compliance features, competing most directly against OpenAI's ChatGPT/GPT models and Google's Gemini in the general-purpose AI assistant and API market.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Claude is the AI assistant product built by Anthropic, an AI safety and research company founded in 2021 by Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei and headquartered in San Francisco. Claude is available as a free consumer chat app on web and mobile, as desktop apps, and as a usage-based API for developers building their own products.

Anthropic offers Claude in multiple model families spanning different speed, cost, and capability levels, used both inside Anthropic's own apps (including the agentic coding tool Claude Code) and by third-party developers accessing the models directly or through cloud partners like Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Claude's consumer apps support conversational chat, code generation, content creation, web search, persistent memory across conversations, and file creation, with paid tiers unlocking higher usage limits and additional tools.

Claude Code extends Claude into agentic software development, letting it read and edit files, run commands, and carry out multi-step coding tasks from a terminal or IDE rather than answering isolated prompts.

For organizations, Team and Enterprise plans add centralized billing, admin controls, SSO and SCIM provisioning, audit logs, configurable data retention, and compliance features aimed at regulated industries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Claude offers a free tier with usage limits, a Pro plan at $17/month billed annually ($20/month billed monthly) with higher limits and access to Claude Code, and a Max plan starting at $100/month for substantially higher usage caps and priority access.

Team plans run $20-25/month per standard seat or $100-125/month per premium seat for organizations of roughly 2-150 people, while Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted based on seat count and API usage. Developers can also access Claude models directly through Anthropic's API on a pay-per-token basis.

features.title: Conversational AI chat
features.description: Web and mobile chat interface for research, writing, brainstorming, and general question answering.

features.title: Claude Code
features.description: An agentic coding tool that lets Claude read, edit, and run code across a codebase from a terminal or IDE.

features.title: Multiple model tiers
features.description: A range of Claude models balancing speed/cost against reasoning depth, selectable by plan and use case.

features.title: Persistent memory
features.description: Retains context across conversations so Claude can reference prior discussions without re-explaining.

features.title: Web search
features.description: Built-in ability to search the web for up-to-date information within a conversation.

features.title: Developer API
features.description: Usage-based API access to Claude models for building custom applications, also available via Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI.

features.title: Enterprise administration
features.description: SSO, SCIM, audit logs, role-based access, and configurable data retention for large organizations.

pros: Free tier available alongside multiple paid tiers for different usage levels
Strong coding and agentic-task capabilities via Claude Code
Enterprise-grade compliance and admin features (SSO, SCIM, audit logs)
Available across web, desktop, mobile, and API, plus major cloud marketplaces
cons: Usage limits on Free and Pro tiers can be restrictive for heavy users
Max and Enterprise tiers are significantly more expensive than entry-level plans
API costs scale with token usage and can be harder to predict than flat subscriptions
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a custom quote

faqs.question: Is Claude free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anthropic offers a free tier of Claude with usage limits for chat, code generation, content creation, and web search on web and mobile.

faqs.question: What is Claude Code?
faqs.answer: Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding tool that lets Claude read, edit, and run code across a project from a terminal or IDE, included with Pro and higher plans.

faqs.question: How much does Claude Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Claude Pro is $17/month when billed annually, or $20/month when billed monthly, and includes higher usage limits and access to Claude Code.

faqs.question: Can businesses use Claude?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anthropic offers Team plans for groups of roughly 2-150 people and custom-quoted Enterprise plans with SSO, SCIM, audit logs, and compliance features for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Who founded Anthropic?
faqs.answer: Anthropic was founded in January 2021 by Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei, along with several other former OpenAI researchers, and is headquartered in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Is there an API for developers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Claude models are available through Anthropic's own API on a usage-based, pay-per-token basis, and are also accessible through Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI.

use_cases.title: Agentic software development

use_cases.title: Enterprise AI deployment
use_cases.description: Building regulated or large-scale AI applications with SSO, audit logs, and compliance controls via Enterprise plans or the API.

features: Everything in Free with higher usage limits

pricing_plans.price: $20-25/month per standard seat; $100-125/month per premium seat

enrichment.short_description: Clay is an AI-powered go-to-market platform that combines 100+ data providers and AI research agents in a spreadsheet-style workflow builder for sales prospecting.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clay is an AI-powered prospecting platform combining 100+ data providers and AI agents in a spreadsheet interface for enrichment and outbound sales.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clay is a go-to-market data and workflow platform built for sales, marketing, and revenue operations teams to research, enrich, and act on lead and account data. The company was founded in June 2017 by Kareem Amin (CEO) and Nicolae Rusan, with Varun Anand joining as a co-founder in 2021, and is headquartered in New York City. Clay began with a broader ambition around programmable data workflows before narrowing its focus, over several product iterations, to become what its founders describe as an integrated development environment for go-to-market teams. The product's core interface is a spreadsheet-like table where each row represents a lead, company, or other record, and each column can run a data-enrichment step: pulling data from over 100 third-party providers (covering firmographic, technographic, contact, intent, and signal data), running web research via an AI agent feature called Claygent that can browse the web and extract specific information, or triggering actions like sending emails, updating a CRM, or posting to an ad platform's custom audience. Clay's pricing is credit-based and tiered: a Free plan includes 500 actions and 100 data credits per month with unlimited seats and up to 200 rows per table; a Launch plan starts around $149-185/month (varying by billing term and credit volume) with roughly 15,000 actions and 3,000 data credits per month, phone-number enrichment, job-change and buying-signal tracking, and up to 50,000 rows per table; a Growth plan starts around $446-495/month with roughly 40,000 actions and 6,000 data credits per month, CRM auto-sync, HTTP API and webhook integrations, and ad-platform audience pushes; and a custom-quoted Enterprise plan adds unlimited ad audiences, SSO, role-based access control, and dedicated support for 200,000+ monthly actions. Clay has raised significant venture funding, including rounds from Sequoia Capital and CapitalG (Google's growth-equity arm), reaching a valuation reported around $3.1 billion in 2026 with annual recurring revenue reported in the range of tens to roughly $100 million and a rapidly growing customer base. Its target users are go-to-market, sales development, and RevOps teams at B2B companies that need to combine multiple data sources and automate outbound research and personalization at scale, rather than individual consumers or non-technical end users, though Clay is explicitly designed to require no traditional coding — workflows are built by chaining together enrichment columns, formulas, and integrations in the spreadsheet interface. Clay competes with tools such as Apollo.io, ZoomInfo, and Clearbit for data enrichment, and with workflow-automation platforms like Zapier or Make for its automation layer, but differentiates itself by combining many data providers behind a single interface with a table-based, low-code workflow builder purpose-built for outbound sales and marketing use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clay is a go-to-market data and automation platform founded in 2017 by Kareem Amin and Nicolae Rusan, with Varun Anand joining as a co-founder in 2021, and headquartered in New York City. It has evolved from a general-purpose data workflow tool into a platform purpose-built for sales, marketing, and RevOps teams doing prospecting and enrichment.

The product centers on a spreadsheet-like table interface, where each row is a record (person, company, or account) and each column runs a data or automation step, letting non-technical users build complex, multi-source enrichment workflows without writing code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clay connects to more than 100 third-party data providers covering firmographic, technographic, contact, and intent/signal data, letting users combine multiple sources per record instead of relying on a single vendor.

Claygent, Clay's AI research agent, can browse the web and extract specific pieces of information into a table column, effectively automating manual research tasks that would otherwise require a human to look things up individually.

Beyond enrichment, Clay supports action steps such as CRM syncing, email campaign integrations, webhook automation, and pushing audiences directly to ad platforms, making it a workflow tool rather than a pure data provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clay uses a freemium, credit-based pricing model. The Free plan includes 500 actions and 100 data credits per month with unlimited seats, while paid plans (Launch, Growth, and Enterprise) scale up action and data-credit allowances alongside additional features like CRM auto-sync and ad-audience pushes.
Paid plans start around $149-185/month for Launch and roughly $446-495/month for Growth, depending on billing term and credit volume, with Enterprise pricing custom-quoted for large-scale usage.

features.description: An AI agent that browses the web to find and extract specific information into a table column.

features.description: Monitors buying signals and job changes to surface timely outbound opportunities.

features.title: CRM auto-sync
features.description: Keeps enriched data synced automatically with CRM systems on higher-tier plans.

features.title: HTTP API & webhook integrations
features.description: Connects Clay workflows to external systems and custom applications.

features.title: Ad-platform audience pushes
features.description: Sends enriched, segmented audiences directly to advertising platforms for targeted campaigns.

AI research agent (Claygent) automates manual lookup tasks
No-code, spreadsheet-familiar interface lowers the barrier to complex workflows
Free plan available for evaluating the platform before committing to a paid tier
cons: Credit-based pricing can become expensive and hard to predict at scale
Paid plans jump significantly in price between tiers
Learning curve for building advanced multi-step workflows
Effective use often requires understanding of the many underlying data providers

faqs.question: Is Clay free to use?
faqs.answer: Clay offers a Free plan with 500 actions and 100 data credits per month and unlimited seats, suitable for evaluating the platform before upgrading to a paid plan.

faqs.question: What is Claygent?
faqs.answer: Claygent is Clay's AI research agent that can browse the web and extract specific information into a table column, automating manual research tasks.

faqs.question: How does Clay's pricing work?
faqs.answer: Clay uses a credit-based model combining 'actions' (workflow steps) and 'data credits' (enrichment lookups), with monthly allowances that scale up across the Free, Launch, Growth, and Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: Do I need to code to use Clay?
faqs.answer: No, Clay is designed as a no-code, spreadsheet-style tool where workflows are built by chaining together enrichment columns, formulas, and integrations.

faqs.question: Who is Clay built for?
faqs.answer: Clay is built for go-to-market, sales development, and revenue operations teams that need to combine data sources and automate outbound prospecting and personalization.

faqs.question: When was Clay founded?
faqs.answer: Clay was founded in 2017 by Kareem Amin and Nicolae Rusan, with Varun Anand joining as a co-founder in 2021, and is headquartered in New York City.

enrichment.short_description: Cleanfeed is a browser-based remote recording platform for broadcast-quality podcast and radio interviews, used by broadcasters like the BBC and NPR.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cleanfeed is a browser-based platform for broadcast-quality remote podcast and radio recording, offering a free Lite tier and a paid Pro tier.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cleanfeed is a browser-based remote audio recording platform built for podcasters, radio producers, and broadcast professionals who need to record interviews with remote guests at broadcast quality. The company was founded by Mark Hills and Marc Bakos and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with the business incorporated in 2015 and generally cited as founded around 2014. Cleanfeed's core technical differentiator is that it runs entirely in a web browser using direct browser-to-browser (peer-to-peer style) audio connections rather than routing audio through a media server, which keeps latency low and lets guests join a recording session via a simple link with no app download, plugin install, or account creation required on their end. The platform is used by major broadcasters, including the BBC and NPR, alongside independent podcasters and commercial production houses, for recording remote interviews destined for radio broadcast or podcast publication. Cleanfeed offers two pricing tiers: Cleanfeed Lite is free and supports browser-based remote recording for up to three participants with no limit on recording length, covering commercial use; Cleanfeed Pro is a paid subscription (listed starting around $23/month standard, with discounted rates around $12/month on annual billing, and further discounts for educational, charity, or personal use) that unlocks multitrack recording (separate, synced WAV files per participant at up to 48kHz), noise removal and echo cancellation, audio repair tools, a live channel mixer, support for groups larger than three participants, higher bitrates (up to 256kbps mono or 320kbps stereo), studio tools such as intros, idents, and jingle beds, privacy controls and personalized invite links, and support for multiple local audio input devices. An Enterprise/Education tier is available for organizations needing five or more Cleanfeed Pro licenses, offering consolidated billing, customization, and branding options through direct sales contact rather than self-serve checkout. Cleanfeed's target users split into two groups: broadcast and radio professionals who need dependable, high-fidelity remote recording integrated into an existing production workflow, and independent podcasters or commercial producers who need an accessible, no-install way to record remote guests without asking them to download separate recording software. It differs from competitors such as Riverside.fm, Squadcast, and Zencastr — which tend to emphasize video recording, automated post-production, and content-repurposing features — by focusing more narrowly on audio recording quality, low latency, and simplicity, reflecting its origins serving traditional radio and broadcast newsrooms before the modern podcasting boom.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cleanfeed is a browser-based remote audio recording platform built for broadcast-quality podcast and radio interviews. Founded by Mark Hills and Marc Bakos and headquartered in London, the company has been used by major broadcasters such as the BBC and NPR as well as independent podcasters and commercial production studios.

Rather than requiring guests to download an app, Cleanfeed uses direct browser-to-browser audio connections, so a host can send a link and start recording with a remote guest with no software installation on the guest's end.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cleanfeed Pro supports multitrack recording, capturing each participant as a separate, synced WAV file at up to 48kHz, along with noise removal, echo cancellation, and audio repair tools for cleaning up problem recordings.

Additional Pro features include a live channel mixer, higher-bitrate encoding (up to 256kbps mono or 320kbps stereo), studio tools like intros, idents, and jingle beds, and privacy controls with personalized guest invite links.
Cleanfeed Lite, the free tier, supports unlimited-length recordings for up to three participants with commercial use permitted, making it usable for basic remote interviews without a subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cleanfeed offers a free Lite plan (up to 3 participants, unlimited recording length) and a paid Pro plan priced from roughly $12/month on annual billing up to around $23-36/month depending on billing term and discount eligibility (education, charity, or personal use).

An Enterprise/Education tier is available for organizations needing five or more Pro licenses, with consolidated billing and branding options available through direct contact with Cleanfeed's sales team.

features.description: Personalized, controlled invite links for guests joining a recording session.

Low-latency, browser-based peer-to-peer audio connections
Free Lite tier supports commercial use with unlimited recording length
Trusted by major broadcasters like the BBC and NPR
cons: Free tier caps sessions at three participants
Focused on audio only, without built-in video recording features some competitors offer
Advanced production and repurposing features are more limited than some newer podcast-first platforms
Multitrack recording and higher-quality audio require the paid Pro tier

faqs.question: Is Cleanfeed free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cleanfeed Lite is a free tier supporting browser-based remote recording for up to three participants with unlimited recording length and commercial use permitted.

faqs.question: Do guests need to install anything to join a Cleanfeed session?
faqs.answer: No, guests join through a web browser via a link, with no app download, plugin, or account required on their end.

faqs.question: What does Cleanfeed Pro add over the free plan?
faqs.answer: Pro adds multitrack recording with separate WAV files per participant, noise removal and echo cancellation, audio repair, a channel mixer, support for more than three participants, and studio tools.

faqs.question: Who uses Cleanfeed?
faqs.answer: Cleanfeed is used by broadcasters including the BBC and NPR, as well as independent podcasters and commercial audio production houses.

faqs.question: Does Cleanfeed offer team or organization pricing?
faqs.answer: Yes, an Enterprise/Education tier is available for organizations needing five or more Cleanfeed Pro licenses, with consolidated billing and branding options via direct contact.

enrichment.short_description: CleanShot X is a Mac-only screenshot and screen-recording app with annotation tools, scrolling capture, GIF recording, and optional cloud sharing.
enrichment.seo_title: CleanShot X: Screenshot & Screen Recording for Mac
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CleanShot X is a Mac screenshot and screen recording app with annotation, scrolling capture, GIF recording, and optional cloud sharing tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CleanShot X is a macOS-only screenshot and screen-recording utility created by developer Luke Oslizlo and a small team based in Poland. The app has no publicly disclosed company registration, funding history, or founding date, and its official site does not publish an About page or corporate history, which is common for small independent Mac software studios; it is nonetheless a well-established, widely reviewed product in the Mac productivity-app category with an active presence on platforms like Product Hunt and Setapp. CleanShot X positions itself as a more capable replacement for macOS's built-in screenshot tool, adding a floating quick-access overlay after each capture, extensive annotation tools (arrows, text, shapes, blur/pixelation), automatic scrolling capture for full web pages or long documents, optical character recognition (OCR) to extract text directly from a screenshot, screen recording saved as video or an optimized GIF, and desktop-icon hiding so captures look clean by default. More recent AI-assisted features include a "smart blur" tool that automatically detects and redacts sensitive information such as email addresses, credit card numbers, and API tokens, AI-generated alt text for accessibility, and a "clean up" function for removing background clutter from a screenshot. CleanShot ships as two purchasable components: the core Mac app, sold as a one-time lifetime license for $29 (covering one Mac, with one year of free updates and an optional $19/year renewal for continued updates), and CleanShot Cloud, an optional service for hosting and sharing captured screenshots, GIFs, and videos via shareable links, available in a Basic tier bundled with the app license (1GB of storage) and a Pro tier priced at $8/month billed annually ($10/month billed monthly) that adds unlimited cloud storage, custom domain branding, password-protected and self-destructing links, and team management with SSO. The app is also distributed through the Setapp subscription bundle (roughly $9.99+/month for access to 240+ Mac apps including CleanShot X) and has been offered as a lifetime deal through AppSumo. CleanShot X does not offer a dedicated standalone free trial of the paid app itself, but purchases are backed by a 30-day money-back guarantee, and Setapp's own trial period provides an alternative way to test the app before committing to a direct purchase. The product requires macOS 10.15 or later and is aimed at designers, developers, support and QA teams, content creators, and any Mac user who takes screenshots or short screen recordings frequently enough to want a faster, more polished workflow than the operating system's default tools provide. It competes most directly with other Mac-focused capture tools such as Shottr, Xnapper, and CloudApp, differentiating itself through the breadth of its annotation and AI-assisted editing features and its combination of a one-time app purchase with an optional recurring cloud-hosting subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CleanShot X is a macOS-only screenshot and screen-recording app created by developer Luke Oslizlo and a small team based in Poland. It's designed as a more capable replacement for macOS's built-in capture tools, adding annotation, scrolling capture, OCR, and screen recording in a single app.

The app has built a strong following among Mac users through word of mouth, reviews, and its presence in the Setapp app bundle, despite the company not publishing formal corporate history or funding details.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capture features include a floating quick-access overlay after each screenshot, extensive annotation tools, automatic scrolling capture for full pages or long documents, and built-in OCR to extract text from an image.
Screen recording can be saved as a video file or an optimized GIF, and the app can automatically hide desktop icons and clutter so captures look clean without manual setup.

AI-assisted features include a smart blur tool that automatically detects and redacts sensitive data like emails, credit card numbers, and tokens, AI-generated alt text for accessibility, and a 'clean up' tool for removing background clutter from a screenshot.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core CleanShot X app is sold as a one-time $29 lifetime license per Mac, including one year of free updates, with an optional $19/year renewal to keep receiving updates after that.

CleanShot Cloud, the optional sharing and hosting service, includes a Basic tier (1GB storage) bundled with the app purchase and a Pro tier at $8/month billed annually ($10/month billed monthly) adding unlimited storage, custom domain branding, password-protected links, and team features. CleanShot X is also available through the Setapp app bundle.

features.description: Uploads captures to shareable links with optional password protection, expiration, and custom domain branding.

Extensive annotation and AI-assisted editing tools beyond macOS's default screenshot tool
Scrolling capture and OCR built directly into the app
30-day money-back guarantee reduces purchase risk
cons: Mac-only; no Windows version
No official company background or founding date published
Unlimited cloud storage and team features require an ongoing Cloud Pro subscription
Update access beyond the first year requires an annual renewal fee

faqs.question: How much does CleanShot X cost?
faqs.answer: The core Mac app is a one-time $29 lifetime license per Mac, including one year of free updates, with an optional $19/year renewal for continued updates.

faqs.question: Is CleanShot X available on Windows?
faqs.answer: No, CleanShot X is built exclusively for macOS and requires macOS 10.15 or newer.

faqs.question: What is CleanShot Cloud?
faqs.answer: CleanShot Cloud is an optional hosting and sharing service for screenshots, GIFs, and videos, with a Basic tier bundled into the app purchase and a paid Pro tier for unlimited storage and team features.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial of CleanShot X?
faqs.answer: There is no dedicated standalone free trial, but purchases are covered by a 30-day money-back guarantee, and the app can also be tried through a Setapp subscription trial.

faqs.question: Who makes CleanShot X?
faqs.answer: CleanShot X is developed by Luke Oslizlo and a small team based in Poland; the company does not publish detailed corporate background information.

enrichment.short_description: Cleanvoice is an AI audio and video editing tool that automatically removes filler words, background noise, mouth sounds, and silences from podcast recordings.
enrichment.seo_title: Cleanvoice: AI Podcast Audio Cleanup Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cleanvoice is an AI tool that automatically removes filler words, noise, and silences from podcast audio and video, with plans from around €11/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cleanvoice is an AI-powered audio and video editing tool focused on automatically cleaning up raw podcast and voice recordings. It was founded in 2022 by Adrian Spataru, who is based in Graz, Austria, and started the company after struggling to find effective automated editing tools while producing his own podcast; frustration with the limitations of existing options for handling filler words and non-English accents was a direct motivation for building Cleanvoice. The product's core function is to take a raw, unedited recording and automatically strip out common editing annoyances — filler words ('um,' 'uh,' and similar), long silences, background noise, and mouth sounds such as lip smacks and breathing — without requiring the user to manually scrub through a waveform in a traditional audio editor. Cleanvoice supports filler-word detection across more than 40 spoken languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Italian, Dutch, Polish, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese, reflecting its founder's original motivation around non-English accent support, and it also offers automatic transcription and works on video files as well as pure audio. Cleanvoice's pricing is credit-based (measured in hours of processed audio) and offered both as one-time, pay-as-you-go credit packs valid for two years (for example, roughly $11 for 5 hours or $45 for 30 hours) and as recurring monthly or annual subscriptions covering set hourly allowances (for example, roughly €11-€90 per month for 10 to 100 hours, with lower effective per-hour rates at higher tiers, and unused credits rolling over up to three times the monthly allotment). A custom-priced plan is available for organizations processing 200 or more hours per month, adding custom API endpoints, priority support, and custom billing, and the company states this enterprise tier is used by more than 30 brands. New users can try Cleanvoice for free without providing a credit card before committing to a paid plan. Cleanvoice's target users are podcast creators, video creators, and audio/video production teams who want to reduce the time spent on manual editing, as well as agencies and platforms that process audio at volume and want API-based automation rather than a manual editing interface for every file. It differentiates itself from broader all-in-one podcast platforms (such as Descript or Riverside.fm, which combine recording, editing, and publishing) by focusing specifically and narrowly on the automated cleanup step of the editing workflow, making it usable as a standalone tool or as a processing step layered on top of recordings made elsewhere, including via its API for developers who want to build cleanup into their own production pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cleanvoice is an AI-powered audio and video cleanup tool founded in 2022 by Adrian Spataru, based in Graz, Austria. It was built to solve a problem the founder faced firsthand while producing his own podcast: manually editing out filler words, silences, and noise was slow, and existing automated tools handled non-English accents poorly.
The product takes raw recordings and automatically removes common editing annoyances, letting creators skip the manual step of scrubbing through a waveform to clean up a recording before publishing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cleanvoice automatically detects and removes filler words, long silences, background noise, and mouth sounds like lip smacks and breathing, and it also offers automatic transcription alongside the cleanup process.

Filler-word detection covers more than 40 spoken languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Italian, Dutch, Polish, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese, and Cleanvoice can process both audio-only files and video.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cleanvoice uses a credit-based pricing model measured in hours of processed audio, available both as one-time credit packs (valid for two years) and as recurring monthly or annual subscriptions with hourly allowances, with unused subscription credits rolling over up to three times the monthly limit.

Plans start around €11/month for 10 hours and scale up to roughly €90/month for 100 hours, with a custom-priced plan for organizations needing 200+ hours per month that adds API endpoints and priority support. New users can try the service for free without a credit card.

features.description: Automatically detects and cuts filler words like 'um' and 'uh' from recordings.

features.title: Background noise reduction
features.description: Removes background noise from raw audio without manual editing.

features.description: Trims long silences and removes mouth sounds such as lip smacks and breathing.

features.title: Multilingual support
features.description: Filler-word detection works across more than 40 spoken languages.

features.title: Automatic transcription
features.description: Generates transcripts alongside the cleanup process.

features.title: Video support
features.description: Processes video files in addition to standalone audio recordings.

features.title: API access
features.description: Custom API endpoints available on the enterprise plan for automated, high-volume processing.

pros: Free trial with no credit card required
Supports filler-word detection in 40+ languages
Unused subscription credits roll over up to 3x the monthly allowance
Works on both audio and video files
cons: Credit-based pricing tied to processing hours can be harder to predict than a flat fee
Focused narrowly on cleanup rather than full recording or publishing features
Per-hour cost is highest on the lowest tier, rewarding higher-volume commitments
Enterprise pricing for 200+ hours/month is not published and requires direct contact

faqs.question: Is Cleanvoice free to try?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cleanvoice offers a free trial with no credit card required before you commit to a paid plan.

faqs.question: How does Cleanvoice pricing work?
faqs.answer: Pricing is credit-based and measured in hours of processed audio, available as one-time credit packs valid for two years or as monthly/annual subscriptions with set hourly allowances, starting around €11/month for 10 hours.

faqs.question: What languages does Cleanvoice support?
faqs.answer: Cleanvoice supports filler-word detection in more than 40 spoken languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Italian, Dutch, Polish, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese.

faqs.question: Can Cleanvoice process video files?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cleanvoice can process video files in addition to standalone audio recordings.

faqs.question: Who founded Cleanvoice?
faqs.answer: Cleanvoice was founded in 2022 by Adrian Spataru, who is based in Graz, Austria, after struggling to find effective automated editing tools for his own podcast.

faqs.question: Does Cleanvoice offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom API endpoints are available on Cleanvoice's enterprise plan for organizations processing 200 or more hours of audio per month.
